coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide and its decoration with high efficiency palladium nanoparticles for direct ethanol fuel cell
coredata.dc:creator: Kakaei, Karim
Rahnavardi, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: For the first time, reduced graphene oxide (RGO) and nitrogen doped RGO (N-RGO) are prepared by the electrochemical procedure and then decorates with palladium nanoparticles via a solvothermal method to obtain Pd/RGO and Pd/NRGO. The as-synthesized electrocatalysts are characterized by transition electron microscopy (TEM), x-ray photoelectron (XPS), energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) techniques. The bare RGO and NRGO electrodes are very active for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and ethanol tolerance, providing the best performance in terms of tafel slope and onset potential. But, the resulting Pd/NRGO electrocatalyst shows outstanding electrocatalytic performance toward both ORR and ethanol oxidation reaction (EOR), including higher peak current density and low tafel slope than that of Pd/RGO, which can be related to high electrochemical active surface area of Pd/NRGO (53 m2 g−1) than that of Pd/RGO (41 m2 g−1). Finally, the fabricated anionic alkaline Membrane-electrode assemblies (MEAs) by using RGO, NRGO, Pd/RGO and Pd/NRGO are studied in single air-breathing direct ethanol fuel cell, as anode and cathode, with solutions of 4 M ethanol and 2 M KOH at room temperature.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical synthesis
N-doped graphene
Ethanol oxidation
Oxygen reduction
Direct ethanol fuel cell

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of graphene, graphene doped nitrogen

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Therefore, innovative methods have been developed in the search of better and new electro-catalysts. Recent efforts in theoretical and experimental are focused on the synthetic procedures to increase Pt surface area [ 7?10] or obtain Pt ?based alternative such as Pd, Cu, Mn, Co electro-catalysts [6,11,12] for ORR or ethanol oxidation reaction (EOR) [13]. Furthermore, various carbon allotrope supports such as graphene, vulcane, carbon quantum dot have been used as a new approach towards the catalysis of the ORR and EOR [ 14?17].Carbon nanostructures and their multifunctional composites have gained remarkable attraction for energy applications because of their high specific surface area, facile decoration, and excellent electrical conductivities [18,19]. Among carbon nanostructure graphene oxide and graphene are extensively applied as an outstanding support for electro-catalysts to obtain desired reaction. For example, it has been investigated that the RGO/GO supported metal nanoparticles can increase the performance of catalysts in oxygen reduction reaction [20], oxygen evolution, alcohol oxidation and hydrogen evolution reaction [21,22]. Additionally, graphene or graphene doped heteroatom [23,24] and Pd-based materials, due to a reduced anion poisoning effect, proved to be active for ORR in alkaline electrolyte [ 25?28].Great progresses have been achieved in Pd-based nanomaterials for olefin hydrogenation studies [36] sensors and biosensors [37,38], catalysis [39], electro catalysis [40,41] and hydrogen storage [42]. Other hand, Pd can serve as a candidate for Pt nanoparticles alternative in the development of catalysts and electrocatalysts with catalytic activity similar to Pt. recently, researchers have presented that the active sites for ORR strongly depend on the surface area of Pd and its supports. Chen et al. have reported a facile photo-assisted chemical reduction approach for the direct formation of Pd onto the porous g-C3N4, which was synthesized by a rapid combustion method and used for ORR as a high stability and methanol tolerance catalyst [12]. The theoretical ORR activity calculation on single Pd atoms, with different loadings, supported on graphitic nitride has been performed by He et al. [43]. They found that the doping of Pd atoms can effectively decompose H2O2, leading to a four-electron mechanism and have lower energy barrier than Pt. Jukk and coworkers [44] reported the production of N-doped graphene by pyrolysis of graphite oxide in the presence of dicyandiamide as nitrogen precursor and modified its surface with Pd NPs by borohydride reduction in order to use in ORR. An in-situ electrochemical reduction of PdO and GO for ORR in KOH electrolyte was investigated by Tathagata et al. [45]. Using density functional theory calculations guided by the oxygen adsorption and vacancy formation energy for Pd NPs as a catalyst could play an important role in ORR performance and long-term durability [46]. Kumar et al. [13] shows that in situ fabrication of Pd NPs on graphene using microwave irradiation technique can improve catalytic activity and sensitivity in ethanol electro-oxidation. Major of the previous reports on graphene materials and graphene decoration metals were investigated in three-electrode compartment system for ORR or EOR. To the best of our knowledge, the present study is the first report on the electrochemical synthesis and application of N-doped graphene based Pd NPs in cathode and anode sides of fuel cell simultaneously.For comparison, Pd NPs supported on RGO (denoted as Pd/RGO) was also synthesized by the same process. The palladium content of all above electrocatalysts is 10 wt%.Fig. 3c shows the TEM image of Pd/RGO, which clearly demonstrates Pd NPs on RGO. TEM image of Pd/NRGO (Fig. 3d) obviously reveals that the Pd nanoparticles are dispersed uniformly on the NRGO sheet. In the statistical analysis, the average particle size was evaluated from 100 particles, while the average particle size from the histogram of size distribution was estimated as 4.92 ? 0.51 nm for Pd/RGO and 3.6 ? 0.52 nm for Pd/NRGO (Fig. 3eandf). The uniformly distribution of Pd NPs on NRGO in comparison to RGO could be related to the d-band centers of supported Pd atoms experience positive shift to the Fermi level as the introduction of N into graphene compared with Pd on RGO, where the d-band centers of Pd atom at NRGO is the most positive one among all supported Pd atoms with the value of 2.40 eV [55,56].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Mechanism of electrochemical exfoliation: which process includes two stages, intercalation and expansion, H2o acts as a radical source and urea acts as a nitrogen source; the intercalation of choline ions leads to complete exfoliation.
(a) FTIR spectra (b) XPS spectra; (c) XRD patterns and (d) EDS of RGO, NRGO, Pd/RGO and Pd/NRGO (e) scale up of EDS of C, N and O.
(a–d) TEM images of RGO, NRGO, Pd/RGO and Pd/NRGO, (e and f) nanoparticle size distribution histogram and (g and h) elemental mapping images of Pd element.
CVs of electrocatalysts in a Ar- and O2-saturated 0.1 M aqueous KOH solution at a scan rate of 50 mVs−1 (a) RGO (b)NRGO, (c) Pd/RGO and (d) Pd/NRGO.
RDE curves of ORR on (a) rGO, (b) N-rGO and (c) Pd/N-rGO catalysts on glassy carbon electrode in O2- saturated 0.1 M KOH solution with various rotation rates at a scanning rate of 5 mV s−1.
(a) Koutecky–Levich plot for Pd/N-rGO at various potentials obtained from fig5(c), plot (b) the n values for RGO, NRGO and Pd/NRGO electrodes at different voltages gained from Koutecky–Levich plot.
(a) LSV ORR performance of RGO, NRGO, Pd/RGO and Pd/NRGO in 0.1 M KOH at 5 mVs−1 (b) The Tafel plot of the ORR on the various electrodes in 0.1 M KOH.
CVs of the (a) RGO and (b) NRGO in presence and absence of ethanol; CVs were obtained after 200 cycles.
CVs of (a) Pd/RGO and (b) Pd/NRGO electrodes in 0.5 M C2H5OH + 1 M KOH collected at a sweep rate from 10 mV s−1 to 250 mV s−1. The current density is obtained according geometric area.
Power density curves and I–V curves obtained at room temperature of the passive ADEFC in the presence of 4 M ethanol and 2 M KOH in anode side and air breathing in cathode side of different MEAs.
Summarize the MEAs, anode catalysts, cathode catalysts and noble metal loading in cathode and anode sides.
Comparison of open circuit voltage (OCV) and maximum power density (Pmax) of direct ethanol fuel cell with different catalysts from other literatures.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide and its decoration with high efficiency palladium nanoparticles for direct ethanol fuel cell

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Maragheh, P.O. Box, 55181-83111, Maragheh, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University Of Maragheh, P.O. Box, 55181-83111, Maragheh, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For the first time, reduced graphene oxide (RGO) and nitrogen doped RGO (N-RGO) are prepared by the electrochemical procedure and then decorates with palladium nanoparticles via a solvothermal method to obtain Pd/RGO and Pd/NRGO. The as-synthesized electrocatalysts are characterized by transition electron microscopy (TEM), x-ray photoelectron (XPS), energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) techniques. The bare RGO and NRGO electrodes are very active for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and ethanol tolerance, providing the best performance in terms of tafel slope and onset potential. But, the resulting Pd/NRGO electrocatalyst shows outstanding electrocatalytic performance toward both ORR and ethanol oxidation reaction (EOR), including higher peak current density and low tafel slope than that of Pd/RGO, which can be related to high electrochemical active surface area of Pd/NRGO (53 m2 g−1) than that of Pd/RGO (41 m2 g−1). Finally, the fabricated anionic alkaline Membrane-electrode assemblies (MEAs) by using RGO, NRGO, Pd/RGO and Pd/NRGO are studied in single air-breathing direct ethanol fuel cell, as anode and cathode, with solutions of 4 M ethanol and 2 M KOH at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:para: Direct ethanol fuel cell (DEFC) is one of the most environmentally friendly and promising energy sources for the future technologies to be used in portable power sources, electric vehicles and transportation application. DEFC has significant advantages over other fuel cells because it uses cheaper and a liquid state reductant at room temperature and pressure, ethanol, as fuel instead of hydrogen. This low temperature fuel cell is easy for transport and handling as well as has high energy density and thermodynamic efficiency [1–3].

The practical application of Pt-based catalysts in fuel cells have hindered in renewable-energy technologies in both anode and cathode sides of DEFC due to the sluggish oxygen reduction reaction [4], alcoholic oxidation and high cost of catalysts [5]. Recently, many researches have made great effort to utilize the high activity, stability and surface area of nanoparticles (NPs) to make a development of fuel cell commercialization. However, application of nanoparticles in fuel cell catalyst was limited by their low physical and chemical stability. Under the standard fuel cell operating conditions, NPs are often loosed their electrochemical catalytic activity during cycling because of oxidizing, dissolving, or detaching from the support and agglomerated into larger particles [6].

Therefore, innovative methods have been developed in the search of better and new electro-catalysts. Recent efforts in theoretical and experimental are focused on the synthetic procedures to increase Pt surface area [7–10] or obtain Pt –based alternative such as Pd, Cu, Mn, Co electro-catalysts [6,11,12] for ORR or ethanol oxidation reaction (EOR) [13]. Furthermore, various carbon allotrope supports such as graphene, vulcane, carbon quantum dot have been used as a new approach towards the catalysis of the ORR and EOR [14–17].

Carbon nanostructures and their multifunctional composites have gained remarkable attraction for energy applications because of their high specific surface area, facile decoration, and excellent electrical conductivities [18,19]. Among carbon nanostructure graphene oxide and graphene are extensively applied as an outstanding support for electro-catalysts to obtain desired reaction. For example, it has been investigated that the RGO/GO supported metal nanoparticles can increase the performance of catalysts in oxygen reduction reaction [20], oxygen evolution, alcohol oxidation and hydrogen evolution reaction [21,22]. Additionally, graphene or graphene doped heteroatom [23,24] and Pd-based materials, due to a reduced anion poisoning effect, proved to be active for ORR in alkaline electrolyte [25–28].

The nitrogen doping into the carbon structure has been carried out by hydrothermal method for NGQDs in ammonia [29], nitrogen plasma treatment [30], wet chemical nitrogen doping [31] solvothermal methods [32]. Different binding energies for N 1s electrons in XPS measurements show that nitrogen may be doped in to the carbon structure as, pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N and graphitic-N (quaternary) centers [33–35]. Since nitrogen electronegativity is higher than carbon, the nitrogen centers have higher electron density and can withdraw electron density from carbons. So that, it can provide the electron withdrawal necessary for activity improvement of the metal nanoparticles [29].

Great progresses have been achieved in Pd-based nanomaterials for olefin hydrogenation studies [36] sensors and biosensors [37,38], catalysis [39], electro catalysis [40,41] and hydrogen storage [42]. Other hand, Pd can serve as a candidate for Pt nanoparticles alternative in the development of catalysts and electrocatalysts with catalytic activity similar to Pt. recently, researchers have presented that the active sites for ORR strongly depend on the surface area of Pd and its supports. Chen et al. have reported a facile photo-assisted chemical reduction approach for the direct formation of Pd onto the porous g-C3N4, which was synthesized by a rapid combustion method and used for ORR as a high stability and methanol tolerance catalyst [12]. The theoretical ORR activity calculation on single Pd atoms, with different loadings, supported on graphitic nitride has been performed by He et al. [43]. They found that the doping of Pd atoms can effectively decompose H2O2, leading to a four-electron mechanism and have lower energy barrier than Pt. Jukk and coworkers [44] reported the production of N-doped graphene by pyrolysis of graphite oxide in the presence of dicyandiamide as nitrogen precursor and modified its surface with Pd NPs by borohydride reduction in order to use in ORR. An in-situ electrochemical reduction of PdO and GO for ORR in KOH electrolyte was investigated by Tathagata et al. [45]. Using density functional theory calculations guided by the oxygen adsorption and vacancy formation energy for Pd NPs as a catalyst could play an important role in ORR performance and long-term durability [46]. Kumar et al. [13] shows that in situ fabrication of Pd NPs on graphene using microwave irradiation technique can improve catalytic activity and sensitivity in ethanol electro-oxidation. Major of the previous reports on graphene materials and graphene decoration metals were investigated in three-electrode compartment system for ORR or EOR. To the best of our knowledge, the present study is the first report on the electrochemical synthesis and application of N-doped graphene based Pd NPs in cathode and anode sides of fuel cell simultaneously.

Now, our objective is to expand our research to obtain graphene doped nitrogen by new, green and fast approach using graphite rod inserting in urea and water by using power supply. On the other hand, we have synthesized graphene doped nitrogen by electrochemical method. In this process, graphite is oxidized to graphene oxide at the anode and is simultaneously doped with urea and then reduced to nitrogen doped graphene in one step without using any additive chemical component. The as-synthesized NRGO nano-sheets can offer enough active sites to grow uniformly Pd particles. Finally NRGO decorated with Pd NPs to obtain Pd/NRGO electrocatalysts by solvothermal (in ethylene glycol) method to be dissolved any material in the solvent. The resulting Pd/NRGO shows outstanding electrocatalytic performance, in comparison to Pd/RGO in anode side and cathode side of passive alkaline direct ethanol fuel cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of graphene, graphene doped nitrogen
ce:section.ce:para: RGO was synthesized using graphite foils as anode and cathode by electrochemical exfoliation method with a separation of 1 cm in H2SO4 (0.5 M). The N-doped graphene was also prepared by using two graphite foils. Choline chloride crystals were used to exfoliate graphite dispersion electrolyte (0.5 M) and 1 M of urea as N-doping source in 100 ml of DI water. Electrochemical exfoliation was performed in both experiments by applying positive voltage (4 V) to the anode graphite electrode in room temperature. When the exfoliation was complete, the suspended product was collected with membrane filter and washed with deionized water.

For the synthesis of NRGO supported Pd as electrocatalysts, 100 mg as-obtained NRGO was dispersed in 200 ml of ethylene glycol and 100 ml deionized water to form a black solution and sonicated for 2 h. Then, 2 ml of PdCl2 solution (5 mg/mL) was added to the above as-prepared aqueous solution and stirred for 30 min. Then, the reaction mixture was moved to a 500 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and maintained at 125 °C for 24 h.
The final solid material (denoted as Pd/NRGO) was then centrifuged, washed 3 times with deionized water and finally dried in a vacuum oven at 45 °C for 2 nights [47].
For comparison, Pd NPs supported on RGO (denoted as Pd/RGO) was also synthesized by the same process. The palladium content of all above electrocatalysts is 10 wt%.

In order to evaluation of the electrochemical performance, The home-made oxygen-breathing DEFC was applied by using 6 catalysts in the anode and cathode sides, in con-junction with the Tokuyama A-006 anion-exchange membrane as shown inFig.1. The reservoir of anode and reticular cathode were fed with 4 M ethanol + 2 M KOH and ambient air, respectively.

A dense anode and cathode paste were made by mixing the catalyst powder with a 5% aqueous solution of PTFE. Generally, an amount of the prepared ink was sonicated and pasted on two identical Ni-foam plates. The 7 MEAs were fabricated by the following catalysts as shown in Table 1 :

Inductively coupled plasma (ICP) was used to measure of bulk Pd content. For these estimation ones dissolved 5 mg of each sample which containing Pd in a mixture of hydrochloric acid and nitric acid (3:1). The solutions were refluxed at 120 °C and finally the percentage of Pd was calculated by ICP according to the standard solution of Pd catalyst. For the calibration curve, the different solution with suitable concentration of Pd (5, 10, 20, 30, 50 and 100 ppm) were ready.

A scanning electron microscope (SEM, Tescan MIRA3) equipped with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) were used for morphology and microstructural characterization. The XRD study was performed by using an XPERT MPD Philips diffractometer with a Cu X-ray source (40 kV, 20 mA). The XRD patterns were obtained at a scanning rate of 1° min−1 with a step size in the 2 scan of 0.02° in the range 5–80°. The FTIR spectrum of the samples was obtained with a Bruker Tensor-27 FTIR spectrometer on a KBr disc over the range 400–4000 cm−1. High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HR-TEM) images were captured on the Schottky field emitter (200 kV).

Generally, the major key factor in graphite exfoliation is efficient intercalation of electrolyte into interlayer space of graphite and the subsequent gas releasing. The intercalation process involves of the large and positively charged (CH3)3NCH2CH2OH+through grain boundaries, intrinsic defect sites and edge sites of a graphite anode, promoting gas evolution at the corresponding sites and large volume expansion. It is well-known that, water splitting can be accomplished at 1.23 V in dilute salt, producing O2 gas at the anode side. While, at the high voltage (e.g., 4 V) discharges water into active groups, such as hydroxyl radicals, at the graphite interfaces [48].
(1)
H 2
O → 2
H +
+
1 2
O 2
+ 2
e −
(2)
H 2
O →
H +
+ O
H ⋅
+
e −

Fig. 1 shows the mechanism of graphite exfoliation and N-doping. As voltage applied to the electrodes the urea solution is decomposed to NH3 and then contributes to N doping.
(3)
C
H 4
N 2
O +
H 2
O → N
H 2
C O O H + N
H 3

Raman and FTIR spectroscopy were used to confirm the synthesis of graphene and n-doped graphene. FTIR was used to analyze the presence of functional group in samples. Fig. 2 a reveals the FTIR spectra of the exfoliated RGO, Pd/RGO, NRGO and Pd/NRGO.

The FTIR curves of RGO show the presence of oxygen containing functional groups, CO and C–O at peak positions 588 cm−1, 1176 cm−1 1703 cm−1, respectively. The presence of fingerprint shoulder peak at 1457 cm−1 was related to N-doped carbon, suggesting successful electro-synthesis of N-doped graphene using urea solution and graphite electrode. The disappearance of absorption bands at 1193 cm−1 and 1728 cm−1 further prove that the oxygen containing moieties on NRGO was reduced. Additionally, an increased very sharply peak intensity at 1457 cm−1 can be attributed to C–N bonding [49,50].

In FTIR spectra of both Pd/RGO and Pd/NRGO, most of the absorption bands are reduced, removed or shifted, demonstrating different profile than those RGO and NRGO. For example, the absorption peaks at 1176 cm−1, 1703 cm−1 and 3450 cm−1 are decreased. This results show strong interaction between palladium nanoparticles and the remaining surface oxygen functional groups on bare RGO and NRGO substrates.

Moreover, according to the FT-IR analysis, a propose reaction pathway for the formation of NRGO is offered as following:
(4)
3
C y
+ 2
H 2
O →
C
2 y
O +
C y
O H + 2
H +
+ 2 e
(5)
C y
O H →
C y
O +
H +
+ e
(6)
(
C y
O )
2
+
N
H 2
C O N
H 2
→
C y
O +
H +
+ e

In order to ensure the presence of heteroatoms and the existence of different bonding configurations of several species in synthesized of RGO, NRGO, Pd/RGO and Pd/NRGO measurements were performed by the XPS method (Fig. 2b). This figure illustrates a C1s peak at about 284.8 eV, O1s region at about 528–538 eV has the high oxygen-containing functional groups which attached on surface for each sample, a N 1s peak beginning around 400 eV for NRGO Pd/NRGO and finally a Pd3d peak at about 338 eV for Pd/RGO and Pd/NRGO [50,51]. This XPS survey spectrum reveals that the catalysts are consisted of Pd element with Pd content around 7 and 6.5% for Pd/RGO and Pd/NRGO. No other oxidation states of palladium were identified at the samples [50].

XRD technique was used to study the phase of the fabricated RGO, Pd/RGO and Pd/NRGO, as shown in Fig. 2c. For RGO, a broad diffraction peak located at around 12–16° and a narrow peak at ca. 24° could be indexed to the (002) and (001) planes, demonstrating that high-degree exfoliation of the graphene containing oxygen functional group is obtained [52,53]. The XRD curve of Pd/RGO catalyst shows several peaks at 2θ of 42.5°, 54.3°, 79.2°, which correspond to the (1 1 1), (2 0 0), and (3 1 1) planes of Pd crystal [20]. XRD pattern of Pd/NRGO displays diffraction peaks same as Pd/RGO with reducing intensity.
Moreover, the broad peak at around 13° which relate to (0 0 2) plane in RGO and Pd/RGO is disappeared. This result suggests that N doped on oxygen groups of graphene is performed.

The mean crystal size of Pd can be easily estimated by using Scherrer formula. From the full-width half-maximum (FWHM) (1 1 1) peak, the average Crystalline size of Pd in Pd/RGO and Pd/NRGO are 6.1 nm and 4.8 nm, respectively [4].
To further study of the composition of electrocatalysts the EDX analysis has been carried out.
The EDX survey spectrum displays that the catalysts are consisted of Pd, C, N, and O elements with high N content up to 9.5 at.% (Fig. 2d and e).

The surface morphological structure of RGO, NRGO, Pd/RGO and Pd/NRGO was further studied by Transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Fig. 3 ). Fig. 3a and b represents the TEM images of RGO, NRGO. The TEM image of the RGO shows thin and wrinkle nanosheets. While, TEM image of NRGO demonstrates wrinkles due to the folding of the sheets [54].

Fig. 3c shows the TEM image of Pd/RGO, which clearly demonstrates Pd NPs on RGO. TEM image of Pd/NRGO (Fig. 3d) obviously reveals that the Pd nanoparticles are dispersed uniformly on the NRGO sheet. In the statistical analysis, the average particle size was evaluated from 100 particles, while the average particle size from the histogram of size distribution was estimated as 4.92 ± 0.51 nm for Pd/RGO and 3.6 ± 0.52 nm for Pd/NRGO (Fig. 3eandf). The uniformly distribution of Pd NPs on NRGO in comparison to RGO could be related to the d-band centers of supported Pd atoms experience positive shift to the Fermi level as the introduction of N into graphene compared with Pd on RGO, where the d-band centers of Pd atom at NRGO is the most positive one among all supported Pd atoms with the value of 2.40 eV [55,56].

The Pd nanoparticle distributions on the RGO and NRGO nanosheets were examined by the EDX techniques. Fig. 3g and h displays the elemental mapping of the Pd metallic NPs. The green images are related to the Pd elements, which indicate that the Pd elements were distributed homogeneously across the NRGO, because of the active sites on NRGO, in comparison to non-distributed Pd particles in RGO, due to lower active site.

Fig. 4 shows the stable cyclic voltammetry (CV) of RGO, Pd/RGO, NRGO and Pd/NRGO in Ar – and O2- saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolytes at 50 mVs−1. No obvious peak can be found in CV obtained from the Ar–saturated aqueous solution for RGO, while a featureless voltammetric current was detected in O2-saturated electrolyte with onset potential approximately −0.25 V (vs Ag/AgCl) (Fig. 4a). However, a well-defined reduction peak have occurred at around −0.40 V, −0.3 V and −0.2 V (vs Ag/AgCl) in the O2-saturated solution NRGO, Pd/RGO and Pd/NRGO, respectively (Fig. 4b and c). These observations indicated that the efficient electrocatalytic activity for these catalysts towards ORR.

In general, the area of Pd oxide reduction in CV curves can be used to calculate the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) of Pd/RGO and Pd/NRGO electrocatalysts in Ar –saturated (Fig. 4 c and d), and the ECSA is calculated according to previous report [20]. The corresponding equation for ECSA is given as following:
(7)
E C S A =
Q s
Q C
× m
×
10
− 4
where QS is the coulombic charge (C) calculated by integrating current peak of PdO reduction, QC is the conversion factor and has been commonly taken as 424 mCcm−2 related to PdO reduction and m is the loadings of Pd catalyst (mg) on the GC surface.

The electrochemically active surface area of the Pd/RGO and Pd/NRGO electrocatalysts were estimated to be 41, and 53 m2g−1 respectively. The reported ECSA in literature for Pd on graphene is nearly 37 m2g−1 [57]. Thus, raising the ECSA to around 53 m2g−1 for Pd/NRGO may be resulted from uniform dispersion of the Pd catalyst NPs [58,59].

To further assess the ORR performance, linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements were next studied in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution using a rotating disk electrode (RDE) at different rotation rates. The working electrode was scanned cathodically at a rate of 5 mV s−1 with varying rotating speed from 400 to 3000 rpm. Fig. 5 a,b, c shows the LSV curves at various rotation rates for (a) rGO, (b) N-rGO and (c) Pd/N-rGO catalysts. As can be seen from these figures the limiting current density enhances with increasing rotation rate for each catalysts. Fig. 5c demonstrates the strong increase in the limiting current density and number of electron transferred for Pd/N-rGO.

Koutecky− Levich plots were applied at various electrode potentials. The slopes of their best linear fit lines were used to calculate the number of electrons transferred (n) on the basis of the Koutecky−Levich equation [60,61]:
(8)
1 j
=
1
j k
+
1
B
ω 0.5
(9)
B = 0.62 n F
(
D
O 2
)
2 / 3
ν
− 1
6
c
O 2
(10)
J k
= nFk
C
O 2

Where J is the measured current density, JK is the kinetic current density, ω is the angular velocity, n is the number of electron transfer, F is the Faraday constant,
D
O 2
is the oxygen diffusion coefficient in the solution (
D
O 2
= 1.9 10−5 cm2 s−1),
C
O 2
is the bulk concentration of dissolved oxygen (
c
O 2
= 1.2 10−6 mol cm−3), C0 is the bulk concentration of O2, ν is the kinematic viscosity of the electrolyte, and k is the electron-transfer rate constant.

The proportionality constant B is known as the Levich B factor. The extrapolation of K-L plots indicates nonzero intercepts and near parallelism of the fitting lines, signifying that the O2 reduction procedure on the Pd/NRGO electrode is under the mixed kinetic-diffusion control in a large range of potentials [62]. Fig. 6 a shows the number of electron transfer values at different voltages (−0.5 V to −0.9 V) for Pd/NRGO. Remarkably, the n values on RGO and NRGO electrodes decreases as potential changes from -1 V to −0.5 V (Fig. 6b). But the average of n value for Pd/NRGO is around 4 with a constant value at different potentials in comparison to 3.6 and 3.3 for NRGO and RGO, respectively.

The LSV method (Fig. 7 a) was also implemented to evaluate the catalytic activity of fabricated electrodes at scanning rates of 5 mV s−1. Usually, Tafel slope derived from LSV curves (Fig. 7b) is measured to estimate the electrochemical kinetics during ORR process. The Tafel slopes were evaluated at low current density (LCD) and high current density (HCD) corresponds to change in the adsorption intermediates from Temkin to Langmurian conditions [17,63].

As shown in Fig. 7b, for the pristine RGO, the values of Tafel are obtained to be 78 mV dec−1 at LCD and 158 mV dec−1 at HCD. After N doping of RGO, the NRGO shows a smaller Tafel values of 71 and 143 mV dec−1, indicating it can more facile overcome the sluggish kinetics process and achieve a high current density with a slight potential increase [61,64]. To further understand the role of the Pd decoration effect onto the graphene surface on the ORR efficiency, we studied LSV for Pd/RGO and Pd/NRGO samples. The Tafel slopes of the Pd/RGO electrocatalysts are about 61 and 130 mV·dec−1 which is near the Pt/C value in LCD and HCD. While, the Tafel slopes of Pd/NRGO are 54 and 121 mV·dec−1, indicating a fast electron transfer of initial adsorbing oxygen adsorption step. The measured Tafel slope for the Pd/NRGO was lower than the Pt/C and Pd/C, electrocatalysts, which yields Tafel slope of 138 mV dec−1 and 158 mV dec−1 [65].

To assess the stability of the catalysts, the CV measurements before and after a long term cycling were examined. It should be noted that, the CV diagrams show no clear change for RGO and NRGO, indicating that these catalysts are stable and ethanol tolerance in an alkaline medium (Fig. 8 a and b).

Fig.(9a and b) presented the CVs of Pd/RGO and Pd/NRGO with different scan rates from 10 mVs−1 to 250 mVs−1 in 0.5 M C2H5OH + 1 M KOH aqueous solutions. The forward peak current density and peak potential were both increased by increasing the scan rate. The forward peaks were attributed to the oxidation of ethanol, and the reverse peaks are related to the oxidation of intermediate products [66]. During the forward scanning, the onset potential (−0.605 V, vs. Ag/AgCl) and mass activity (1422.5 mA·mg−1Pd) of Pd/NRGO were much better than that Pd/RGO, which its onset potential is −0.654 V, vs. Ag/AgCl and mass activity is (956.5 mA·mg−1Pd). Moreover, As shown inFig. (9a and b), If/Ib value for Pd/NRGO (3.9) is much larger than that Pd/RGO (2.1) electrocatalyst. This result demonstrates that Pd/NRGO has better poisoning-tolerant behavior, better catalytic efficiency and more effective removal of carbonaceous intermediate species on the surface of electrode [67].

The home-made fabricated passive ADEFC device different MEAs was operated at room temperature using 4 M ethanol and 2 M KOH as a fuel and ambient air. The cell polarization and power density plots are shown in Fig. 10 . The presence of hydroxyl groups in the fuel has been investigated in the literature [68]. The gained around 0.80 V OCV in ADEFC is in good agreement with reported data in the scientific literature for passive DEFC [68]. MEA1 and MEA2 have identical cathodes with metal free electro catalyst (RGO) but their anode side is dissimilar and contains Pt/C for MEA1 and Pd/RGO in MEA2. This result recommends that the Pd/RGO represents an enhanced electro-catalytic activity for the oxidation of ethanol in alkaline medium in comparison to the Pt/C (E-TEK) catalyst.

MEA3 and MEA4 have also the same metal free electro catalyst cathodes (NRGO) and differ from the cathodes of MEA1 and MEA2. The anode side of MEA3 and MEA4 are different and contains Pd/RGO for MEA3 and Pd/NRGO for MEA4. The results demonstrates that graphene doped nitrogen not only can help the oxygen reduction but can promote the uniform dispersion of the Pd nanoparticles and increasing the electrochemical active site for ethanol oxidation. Thus, the performance of MEA4 is higher than those MEA1, MEA2 and MEA3.

In MEA5 and MEA6, both anode and cathode were fabricated using the same catalyst, but they were different (i.e Pd/RGO for MEA5 and Pd/NRGO for MEA6). MEA7 shows identical standard Pt/C cathode and anode catalyst for comparing with other MEAs. The MEA6 has the height efficiency than all samples. So we can say that, the introduction of nitrogen in graphene surface not only can enhance the ORR performance and ethanol tolerance but also can increase the distribution and ECSA of Pd nanoparticles.

Moreover, the OCV and power density of our MEAs was compared with different catalysts in Table 1. It can be seen that the present work is one of the materials with the lowest ethanol electro-oxidation potential in the previous reports, listed in Table 2 . The obtained high performance may be attributed to the unique structures of NRGO with high specific surface area than RGO, which providing more active sites and allowed easy access of reactants to the electrode surfaces.

In summary, we have synthesized N-doped graphene nanosheets by using a simple and ecofriendly electrochemical method and then decorated with PdNPs. From the comparative study between four dissimilar electrocatalysts, it was understood that Pd/NRGO has the highest efficiency toward ethanol oxidation and ORR in an alkaline medium. The main credit behind the better efficiency of Pd/NRGO goes to their higher electrochemical surface area, and nitrogen doping. The fabricated Pd/NRGO was employed for the fabrication of MEA electrocatalysts in ADEFC. The maximum power output obtained (31.5 mW cm2) was stronger that the reported in the literatures. This work demonstrates the performance of the Pd on NRGO and RGO based MEA is much higher and the most effective than commercial Pt/C based MEA.
Karim Kakaei: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing - original draft, preparation, Supervision. Mohammad Rahnavardi: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mesoporous nanostructured CoFe–Se–P composite derived from a prussian blue analogue as a superior electrocatalyst for efficient overall water splitting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. He, B. Cui, B. Hu, J. Liu, K. Tian, M. Wang, Y. Song, S. Fang, Z. Zhang, Q. Jia, Mesoporous Nanostructured CoFe-Se-P Composite Derived from a Prussian Blue Analogue as a Superior Electrocatalyst for Efficient Overall Water Splitting, ACS Applied Energy Materials, 1 (2018) 3915-3928.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Comparison of ethanol electro-oxidation on Pt/C and Pd/C catalysts in alkaline media

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Ma, D. Chu, R. Chen, Comparison of ethanol electro-oxidation on Pt/C and Pd/C catalysts in alkaline media, International Journal of Hydrogen Energy, 37 (2012) 11185-11194.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Decoration of graphene oxide with Platinum Tin nanoparticles for ethanol oxidation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Kakaei, Decoration of graphene oxide with Platinum Tin nanoparticles for ethanol oxidation, Electrochimica Acta, 165 (2015) 330-337.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot synthesis of Palladium Silver nanoparticles decorated reduced graphene oxide and their application for ethanol oxidation in alkaline media

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Kakaei, M. Dorraji, One-pot synthesis of Palladium Silver nanoparticles decorated reduced graphene oxide and their application for ethanol oxidation in alkaline media, Electrochimica Acta, 143 (2014) 207-215.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pd and Pt–Ru anode electrocatalysts supported on multi-walled carbon nanotubes and their use in passive and active direct alcohol fuel cells with an anion-exchange membrane (alcohol=methanol, ethanol, glycerol)

Zhiani, Pd and Pt-Ru anode electrocatalysts supported on multi-walled carbon nanotubes and their use in passive and active direct alcohol fuel cells with an anion-exchange membrane (alcohol=methanol, ethanol, glycerol), Journal of Power Sources, 190 (2009) 241-251.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Use of Pd-Pt loaded graphene aerogel on nickel foam in direct ethanol fuel cell

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.H.A. Tsang, D.Y.C. Leung, Use of Pd-Pt loaded graphene aerogel on nickel foam in direct ethanol fuel cell, Solid State Sciences, 75 (2018) 21-26.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation, characterisation and application of Pd/C nanocatalyst in passive alkaline direct ethanol fuel cells (ADEFC)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.M. Modibedi, T. Mehlo, K.I. Ozoemena, M.K. Mathe, Preparation, characterisation and application of Pd/C nanocatalyst in passive alkaline direct ethanol fuel cells (ADEFC), International Journal of Hydrogen Energy, 40 (2015) 15605-15612.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Influence of graphene oxide on the ethanol permeability and ionic conductivity of QPVA-based membrane in passive alkaline direct ethanol fuel cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Zakaria, S. Kamarudin, S. Timmiati, Influence of graphene oxide on the ethanol permeability and ionic conductivity of QPVA-based membrane in passive alkaline direct ethanol fuel cells, Nanoscale research letters, 14 (2019) 28.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Energy efficient role of Ni/NiO in PdNi nano catalyst used in alkaline DEFC

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Dutta, J. Datta, Energy efficient role of Ni/NiO in PdNi nano catalyst used in alkaline DEFC, Journal of Materials Chemistry A, 2 (2014) 3237-3250.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Hou, S. Wang, W. Jin, Q. Jiang, L. Sun, L. Jiang, G. Sun, KOH modified Nafion112 membrane for high performance alkaline direct ethanol fuel cell, International Journal of Hydrogen Energy, 36 (2011) 5104-5109.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Balancing the Electron Conduction and Mass Transfer: Effect of Nickel Foam Thickness on the Performance of an Alkaline Direct Ethanol Fuel Cell (ADEFC) with 3D Porous Anode, International Journal of Hydrogen Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhang, P. Leung, F. Qiao, L. Xing, C. Yang, H. Su, Q. Xu, Balancing the electron conduction and mass transfer: Effect of nickel foam thickness on the performance of an alkaline direct ethanol fuel cell (ADEFC) with 3D porous anode, International Journal of Hydrogen Energy, (2020).
